# 2026 archive (fabricated — for stratified-sampling fixture)

This file simulates a year-archive with ~50 short posts concatenated. Each post has its own `## <title>` heading. Pipeline should detect this as a MULTI-DOCUMENT file and use Stratified Sampling.

## 2026-01-02 — Opening the year

A distinctive move for this fictional thinker: asking "what are we measuring?" before engaging with any proposal. Simple question, hard to bluff.

The year opens with a claim: most measurement in organizations is proxy measurement. The proxy outlives the purpose. Once a number exists, people optimize for it and forget what it was meant to approximate.

## 2026-01-09 — The proxy problem, continued

Expanding on last week's proxy thesis. Example: NPS scores. Designed to measure customer love; used to measure rep performance. The delta between those two uses is where the proxy goes rotten.

## 2026-01-16 — Three kinds of certainty

Noticed three kinds of certainty in the wild: certainty from evidence (high-value), certainty from authority (medium-value — can be wrong but at least sourced), certainty from vibe (worthless and dangerous). The third gets treated like the first more often than we admit.

## 2026-01-23 — "Why don't we just?"

Every meeting I've ever been in has at least one "why don't we just" moment. The thing after "just" is never just. If it were just, someone would have already done it. "Just" is the word we use to dismiss the work we don't want to do.

## 2026-01-30 — Compounding invisibility

Good work compounds invisibly. Bad work compounds visibly. That's why most organizations drift toward visible bad work: the compounding is legible, attributable, fundable.

## 2026-02-06 — The third-option instinct

When a conversation reaches a binary fork, reach for the third option. Not as a compromise — as a reframe. "If the choice feels binary, the frame is wrong."

## 2026-02-13 — Reading people

Spent most of the week reading people, not memos. Memos lie by omission. People lie too, but they lie differently each time, and the pattern of lies tells you what they're actually protecting.

## 2026-02-20 — The question I ask most

"What are you optimizing for?" More than any other question, this one changes the temperature of a conversation. Usually because the answer is "I don't know" and we get to start over.

## 2026-02-27 — Unfalsifiable beliefs

When someone can't name what would change their mind, the belief isn't load-bearing on evidence. It's load-bearing on identity. You can't argue identity with evidence; you have to let the identity-cost come due some other way.

## 2026-03-06 — Mid-year already

Week 10 of 2026. Still asking the same three questions. They don't get old because the answers don't get old either.

## 2026-03-13 — Process as proxy

Process-as-proxy disease: the process was designed to achieve X, organization preserves the process after X is irrelevant, eventually reorganizes around the process itself. Grove noticed this. Still applies.

## 2026-03-20 — Small interventions

A small intervention at the right place beats a large intervention at the wrong place. Finding the right place is the whole skill. Most interventions get mislocated because we choose where to intervene based on where we can see, not where the leverage is.

## 2026-03-27 — Spring reset

Every quarter, reset three things: the questions, the tools, the people. Quarter reset is enough to adapt; year-long reset is stagnation.

## 2026-04-03 — Reading the room

The skill of "reading the room" gets worse with seniority, not better. Juniors have to read the room. Seniors expect the room to read them. The second condition is pleasant and misleading.

## 2026-04-10 — Systems are stories

Every system is a story told in machinery. When the story stops matching reality, the system breaks — but the machinery keeps running for a while before anyone notices.

## 2026-04-17 — Mid-file sample chunk

This is roughly the midpoint of the year. Stratified sampling subagents would pick up material from this section.

## 2026-04-24 — Compounding, revisited

Returning to January's theme. Compounding requires three things: reinvestment, time, and not-interrupting. Most organizations fail at the third. Every reorganization is an interruption.

## 2026-05-01 — Measurement again

Four months in and still thinking about proxy measurement. The number of times I've had to pull "but what were we trying to measure?" out of a conversation is not small.

## 2026-05-08 — The third option, again

Revisited the third-option instinct. Works almost always in strategy, almost never in ethics. Ethics is usually binary by construction.

## 2026-05-15 — What would change your mind

Getting better at asking this. Getting worse at having a good answer myself. Hmm.

## 2026-05-22 — Reading people again

Had a week of 1:1s. Reading people is still the thing. Memos are still lying.

## 2026-05-29 — Quarterly check

End of Q2. Still optimizing for the same three questions. Tools: changed. People: two additions, one departure. The process itself: unchanged, which is suspicious.

## 2026-06-05 — Mid-year reflection

Half a year. The questions hold up. The world did not end. Small things that should not have worked did. Large things that should have worked did not. Compounding invisibly vs visibly, as always.

## 2026-06-12 — Late-spring drift

Noticing drift in the process. Questions I've asked 100 times start to feel automatic — dangerous. Going to rotate the phrasing next week.

## 2026-06-19 — Process rotation experiment

Tried "what are we actually trying to accomplish" instead of "what are you optimizing for." Different question, same instinct. Landing differently.

## 2026-06-26 — End of H1

Half-year done. The thesis holds: measurement, falsification, reframe. Three moves, one mind. Continuing.

## 2026-07-03 — H2 begins

New quarter, same questions, different audience. The questions don't age. The contexts do.

## 2026-07-10 — Organizational physics

Organizations have physics. Inertia, gravity, momentum. You can't argue with the physics; you have to work with it or route around it. Most change initiatives fail because they argue with the physics.

## 2026-07-17 — The reframe muscle

Reframe muscle gets stronger with exercise. If you don't practice it, it atrophies. Daily reframes, even on trivial things, are resistance training for the real thing.

## 2026-07-24 — Reading and writing

Read more than you write. Write more than you talk. Talk less than you listen. Most days I invert this order and the day goes worse.

## 2026-07-31 — End of July

A month of questions. The three-question toolkit still earns its keep.

## 2026-08-07 — The proxy again

Third time returning to proxy measurement. That's a signal. When a theme recurs, it's either a pattern or an obsession. Sometimes both.

## 2026-08-14 — Late summer

Tactical stuff this week. Nothing novel.

## 2026-08-21 — The frame

Catching myself reaching for the same frames too often. Time to read outside my lane.

## 2026-08-28 — Reading outside the lane

Spent the week reading marketing literature (Kotler's Marketing 5.0), design (Rams retrospective), philosophy (Seneca letters). Different frames, same problem shape.

## 2026-09-04 — September

Labor Day weekend. Rest day for the questions.

## 2026-09-11 — Returning

Back to the loop. What are you optimizing for? What would change your mind? Is the choice binary or is the frame wrong?

## 2026-09-18 — New material

New book announcement from Seth Godin: "The Knot". Interesting move — problem vs situation. Worth adopting.

## 2026-09-25 — Problem vs situation

Adopting the problem/situation distinction. Cleaner taxonomy than "actionable vs inactionable." Gravity is a situation. The moon is a problem.

## 2026-10-02 — October check

Moved into a new quarter. Same three questions, one borrowed new distinction. The toolkit grows slowly.

## 2026-10-09 — Compounding, third time

Third or fourth return to compounding. Clearly an obsession. Logging it.

## 2026-10-16 — End of October approach

Final read cycle closing — stratified sampling would pick up material from around here for the "closing" chunk.

## 2026-10-23 — Year-end drift

Starting to think about year-end. Reminder to self: the point of year-end is to rest, not to reorganize.

## 2026-10-30 — Halloween

No content today. Took the day off. The questions can wait.

## 2026-11-06 — November

A month away from year-end. The year held together. The questions held up. The frame continues to work.

## 2026-11-13 — Measurement, fourth time

Measurement is clearly the strongest recurring theme. Four returns in 11 months. Will write the essay this quarter.

## 2026-11-20 — Ahead of the end

Two months of 2026 left. The toolkit: unchanged. The problems: same shape, different wrapping.

## 2026-11-27 — Post-Thanksgiving

Rest week. The questions get better with rest.

## 2026-12-04 — Final stretch

Four weeks left in the year. Wrapping the thesis.

## 2026-12-11 — Essay in progress

Drafting the measurement essay. Five passes in. Getting tighter each pass.

## 2026-12-18 — Essay shipped

Shipped the measurement essay. Reframed the proxy problem as "the goal outliving the measurement." Different framing, same insight.

## 2026-12-25 — Holiday

Nothing today. Rest and family.

## 2026-12-29 — End of the year

Closing the archive. The three questions held up across 50+ posts. The measurement theme recurred four times. The reframe instinct sharpened. The proxy/situation distinction joined the toolkit.

Next year: the questions continue. The toolkit grows if and only if the new items earn their keep. Most won't.

End 2026 archive.
